paragraphs.body: Appearance​ The intoxicating smell of Jolen face bleach. The grittiness of the mixture after not properly blending it with the developer. The first strands of golden hair glowing under the bathroom light. I was fifteen. You always noticed everything. It was the first sign, but not the last.

Body​ Nineteen piercings, forty-four tattoos. A declaration etched into my skin. The sting of the needle, the permanence of ink. I was making myself mine. You never asked what they meant. Maybe you were afraid of the answer.

Clothes​ A dress. A skirt. The wrong fabric against my skin. “Thank God it was just a phase,” I hear you think. But it wasn’t. It never was. I spent years performing, wearing what was expected, suffocating inside soft folds and floral prints. Now, I am free. And you are looking away.

Dysphoria​ Looking in the mirror, a gut-wrenching feeling coils in my stomach. Feeling foreign in my own body, feeling whole only when bound, when hidden, when unseen. The fear of being perceived incorrectly. The exhaustion of correcting those who refuse to see.

Expectations​ Don’t cut your hair too short. Don’t make yourself look different. Don’t change what God gave you. Don’t mark yourself permanently. Don’t wear those clothes. Don’t forget that people are always watching. Don’t love who you love. Don’t expect me to understand. Don’t expect me to change.

Father​ I was seventeen when you said if I got top surgery, I would no longer be welcome in your house. You said it quietly, as if testing how much you meant it. I stayed silent, staring at myself, wondering how a home could become conditional so easily.

annotations.text: I was seventeen when you said if I got top surgery, I would no longer be welcome in your house. You said it quietly, as if testing how much you meant it. I stayed silent, staring at myself, wondering how a home could become conditional so easily.
annotations.comment: This is an extremely strong passage that gives your reader a powerful sense of an exact situation and allows you to develop your own thoughts and reactions to it.

paragraphs.body: Gender​ Hindu texts speak of the third gender, acknowledged and revered for centuries. Yet, in your eyes, I am something else—something that does not belong to history, only to defiance.

Hair​ Long. Then short. Streaks of bleach blonde. Then brightly colored. Then short again. The look on your face the first time I cut it after coming out. It wasn’t about the hair. It was about what it meant. About what I meant. The intoxicating smell of Manic Panic green dye clung to my hair, marking a milestone in my metamorphosis.

Identity​ Me: I just wish you could understand that I’m not doing this to hurt you.​ Mom: Then why does it feel like you are? Why do you have to change so much?​ Me: Because this is who I’ve always been. You just never saw me.​

Mom: I see you. But I don’t know if I recognize you anymore.​ Me: Maybe that’s the problem. You’re looking for someone who never existed.​ Mom: I don’t know how to let go of that.​ Me: You don’t have to let go of me. Just let go of who you thought I would be.

Judgment​ The way you pause before saying my name, how you never trip over the old pronouns, only the new ones. A hesitation that says everything. I wonder if you ever practice saying it when I’m not around, if you try to get it right in the privacy of your thoughts.

Knowledge​ What do you fear most about accepting me for who I truly am? What do I fear most about you never doing so? The weight of expectation is heavy. I have carried it for so long that I sometimes mistake it for my own. But I am learning to put it down, piece by piece, even if it means leaving part of you behind.

Love​ Despite it all, I am still yours. And maybe that is why it hurts. Love is not always understanding, but it should at least be trying. I wonder if you know that I still love you, even when I feel like I am losing you. Even when I wonder if you have already lost me.
Memory​ This piece I’ve written is an offering, a door left slightly open. You may never step through it, but at least now you know it is there.

annotations.comment: Hello, The focusing central idea at the heart of this essay continues to be how your gender identity has affected the relationship between you and your parents. The greatest strength of this piece, which is a great strength in all of your writing, is how candid and thoughtful you are about your own identity and the relationships. These relationships are clearly extremely important to you, but sometimes that kind of importance can make a subject even more difficult to write about. The word that stands out the most in this piece is you and its variants. Clearly, much of your writing here is directed towards your parents, so the you is often you, Mom or you, Dad, but you are also doing a great job of considering their perspectives, as well as interactions between each of you, so, sometimes, the you is you, Adrian. That’s really interesting to consider. It makes me really feel the importance of these relationships. As a reader, I feel as though the you is more than one person, the you is us all, and we are all responsible for one another. In simplest terms, the audience of this piece is, of course, your parents, but because of the skill you use and the care you take to present this piece of family history, the audience is much more far reaching. It is for all of the parents who don’t understand their children (in other words, all parents). The audience is parents who want their children to be different than they actually are, and the purpose of the piece is to show this audience that we cannot change who we are. We have to accept each other. You have to accept you and you have to accept yourself. There is an author named Thich Nhat Hanh who wrote a book called Interbeing. The main theme of the book is something like all things are all other things—we don’t stop being at the edge of our skin—I am the clouds, I am the rain, I am the soil, and you are these things, as well. It’s been almost three decades since I read it, so that’s sort of a simple, silly explanation of a beautiful book, but what I’ve learned in reading your piece is that you, in many ways, already view the world as such. You are your parents and they are you, and they are your tattoos and you are their clothes and their home. Though you don’t say it, as such, I feel it in reading your work. I hope I’m not reading too much into this. Thanks for Sharing with Me,
